source_book: NCERT Class 11 Physics Part I Ch.8 + HC Verma Ch.6

real_world_anchor.primary: A heavy door hangs from two small hinges. Those hinges must be pushing on the door — but in which direction?
real_world_anchor.secondary: A gate swings on its pin joint. The gate experiences a horizontal pull when you push it and a vertical pull from gravity. The hinge absorbs both.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: A rod pinned to a wall with a load hanging at the free end. The pin has to react somehow — where does its force point?

constraints: Hinge constrains position — cannot constrain rotation
Hinge force can be in ANY direction in the plane (unlike N which is perpendicular, unlike T which is a pull)
Find H and V from equilibrium: sum(Fx) = 0, sum(Fy) = 0
Hinge can both push and pull — it is a pin joint
For multi-unknown problems, use torque equation sum(tau) = 0 about the hinge to eliminate the hinge force and solve other unknowns first

STATE_1.title: The Door Hinge — Heavy Door, Just Two Pins. How?

scene_composition.text:
The door weighs 196 N downward.
The hinge clearly supports it —
but in which direction?

tts_sentences.text_en: A heavy door hangs from just two small pins in the wall.
The door weighs one hundred ninety six newtons. Gravity pulls it straight down.
The hinge must be pushing back on the door, otherwise it would fall.
But in which direction does the hinge force point? That is the question we answer today.

STATE_2.title: Hinge Is Different — It Can Push or Pull in ANY Direction

scene_composition.left_label: String or Normal — one direction only
scene_composition.right_label: Hinge — any direction, push OR pull

left_scene.label: String can only pull along its length

left_scene.text:
Normal reaction and string
each act along ONE fixed direction.

right_scene.text:
A hinge can push or pull
in whatever direction
equilibrium demands.

scene_composition.text: Three common constraints — very different freedoms

A hinge is a pin joint.
It fixes the position of one point
but imposes no direction on the force.

tts_sentences.text_en: There are three common types of constraints — strings, normal reactions, and hinges.
A string can only pull, and only along its own length.
A normal reaction can only push, and only perpendicular to the contact surface.
A hinge is different. It can exert a force in any direction — push or pull, as long as equilibrium needs it. Tap continue.

STATE_3.title: How to Find Hinge Force — Equilibrium Equations

scene_composition.equation_expr: sum(Fx) = 0 -> H = {F_ext} N    sum(Fy) = 0 -> V = {W} N    F_hinge = sqrt(H^2 + V^2) = {Math.sqrt(F_ext*F_ext + W*W).toFixed(1)} N

tts_sentences.text_en: The method has two steps. First, split the hinge force into a horizontal component H and a vertical component V.
Use horizontal equilibrium. Sum of forces in x equals zero. That gives you H.
Use vertical equilibrium. Sum of forces in y equals zero. That gives you V.
Combine with Pythagoras. F equals the square root of H squared plus V squared. The angle is arctan of V over H.

STATE_4.title: Special Case — No External Load, Just Weight

scene_composition.text:
With no external horizontal load,
H = 0 and V = W.
Hinge force collapses to a
simple vertical support.

tts_sentences.text_en: Now remove the external horizontal load. Only the rod's weight remains.
Horizontal equilibrium gives H equals zero. There is nothing horizontal to balance.
Vertical equilibrium gives V equals W, just forty newtons upward.
The hinge force becomes purely vertical in this case. That is the limit everyone starts with, but it is only a special case.

STATE_5.title: You Control It — Change Load, Watch Hinge Force Rotate

scene_composition.equation_expr: H = {F_ext} N    V = {W} N    F_hinge = {Math.sqrt(F_ext*F_ext + W*W).toFixed(1)} N    theta = {(Math.atan2(W, F_ext) * 180 / Math.PI).toFixed(1)} deg

tts_sentences.text_en: Drag the sliders. Change the weight and the external load, and watch the hinge force rotate.
Set the external load to zero. The hinge force becomes pure vertical, just balancing the weight.
Set the rod weight to zero. The hinge force becomes pure horizontal, just balancing the external load.
This is the real power of a hinge — it provides exactly whatever direction of force equilibrium demands.

annotations.text: The vector tip marks (H, V). Its angle changes with the load.

regeneration_variants.label: Ladder Hinged at the Base
regeneration_variants.physical_world: A ladder hinged to the wall at the bottom, its top resting against a smooth ceiling. Find the hinge force at the base.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Hinge force found from sum(Fx) = 0 and sum(Fy) = 0. Magnitude is sqrt(H^2 + V^2).
regeneration_variants.scene_hint: Ladder body. Hinge at bottom-left against the wall. Smooth ceiling contact at the top, giving a horizontal N. Weight at the center.

regeneration_variants.label: Crane Arm (Derrick)
regeneration_variants.physical_world: A horizontal beam hinged to a wall at its left end. A rope runs from the wall to the beam to hold it up. A heavy load hangs at the right end. Find the hinge force.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Many forces act on the beam. The hinge absorbs whatever H and V equilibrium requires.
regeneration_variants.scene_hint: Horizontal rod. Hinge at the left end. Tension rope from the wall to the middle of the beam. Load W pulling down at the right end.

regeneration_variants.label: Pinned Support on a Bridge
regeneration_variants.physical_world: A simply supported beam has a pin at one end and a roller at the other. A truck drives across. The pin reacts with both horizontal and vertical components; the roller only vertical.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Same equilibrium method — just now the hinge is at one end and the roller constraint at the other.
regeneration_variants.scene_hint: Horizontal beam. Pin (triangle symbol) at left. Roller (circle on ground) at right. Point load somewhere in the middle.

epic_c_branches.misconception: The hinge force is always vertical — it only supports the weight.

EPIC_C_1.title: Your Belief: Hinge Provides Vertical Support Only

scene_composition.right_label: Reality: F_hinge has H and V

left_scene.text:
Missing the horizontal piece.
The system would slide sideways.

right_scene.label: F_hinge = 50 N at 53 deg from horizontal

right_scene.text:
H = 30 N, V = 40 N.
Both components are needed for equilibrium.

scene_composition.text: Myth: 'Hinge just holds the weight up.'

Fix: write BOTH sum(Fx)=0 and sum(Fy)=0.
If the problem has any horizontal push or pull,
the hinge must carry that horizontally too.

tts_sentences.text_en: If the hinge could only push vertically, nothing would balance the horizontal load. The whole system would slide sideways.
The horizontal component of the hinge force balances any horizontal load in the problem.
The vertical component balances the weight plus any vertical load.
Both components act simultaneously. Thinking of the hinge as only vertical is wrong.

epic_c_branches.misconception: A hinge force is the same as a normal reaction.
trigger_phrases: hinge is just the normal force
hinge and normal are the same

left_scene.text:
Always perpendicular to contact surface.
Only pushes — never pulls.
Direction fixed by surface geometry.
Vanishes if the body lifts off.

right_scene.text:
Any direction — no constraint.
Can push or pull.
Direction determined by equilibrium.
Always present as long as the pin is engaged.

scene_composition.text: Both are contact forces — but different geometry.

Try this test:
Can the body leave the surface?
- Yes -> normal reaction (disappears).
- No -> hinge force (still there).

tts_sentences.text_en: The normal reaction can only act perpendicular to the contact surface, and it can only push.
The hinge force can act in any direction, and it can push or pull.
A normal reaction disappears the instant contact is lost. A hinge force remains as long as the pin joint is intact.
Both are contact forces, but their geometry and freedom are completely different.

epic_c_branches.misconception: The rod's weight acts at its free end (or at the hinge), not at the centre of mass.
trigger_phrases: weight acts at the end of the rod
where to put the rod weight

EPIC_C_1.title: Your Belief: Weight at the Free End

scene_composition.text:
Many students draw W at the tip —
which makes the torque wrong
and the hinge force wrong.

tts_sentences.text_en: A common mistake is to place the rod's weight at its free tip, because that is where the load usually hangs.
But the weight of a uniform rod acts at its centre of mass — the geometric middle, not the tip.
Placing W at the wrong point does not change force equilibrium much, but it changes the torque equation.
And when you solve multi-unknown problems with torques, wrong W position gives wrong hinge force.

EPIC_C_2.title: Correct Placement: Weight at the Centre of Mass

scene_composition.text:
Rule: draw the rod's weight at L/2.
Draw any hanging load at its actual
attachment point. The torque equation
about the hinge then gives the correct
unknown force.

tts_sentences.text_en: For a uniform rod, the weight always acts at its centre of mass — exactly at L over 2 from either end.
Any external load hangs at its real attachment point. Do not merge the two.
Now apply sum of torques equals zero about the hinge. The hinge force itself has zero moment arm, so it drops out of the equation.
That is how the torque trick lets you solve for unknown loads first, then come back and find the hinge force from force equilibrium.

epic_c_branches.misconception: Only sum(Fx) = 0 and sum(Fy) = 0 are needed; the torque equation is optional.

EPIC_C_1.title: Why the Torque Equation Is Often Essential

scene_composition.text: Unknowns: H, V, and T    (3 values)

left_scene.text:
sum(Fx) = 0
sum(Fy) = 0

Two equations, three unknowns.
System is UNDER-DETERMINED.
You cannot solve.

right_scene.text:
sum(Fx) = 0
sum(Fy) = 0
sum(tau)_hinge = 0

Three equations, three unknowns.
Torque about the hinge eliminates
H and V, so T solves first.

scene_composition.equation: sum(tau)_hinge = 0  ->  T * L_T - W * (L/2) - F_ext * L = 0

scene_composition.text:
Pick the hinge as the axis.
The hinge force has zero moment arm
about itself, so it drops out.
Only the external unknowns remain.

tts_sentences.text_en: In real problems the rod is held by a hinge AND a rope or external support. You have three unknowns — H, V, and the rope tension T.
Two force equations alone give you two conditions for three unknowns. The system is underdetermined.
Add the torque equation about the hinge. The hinge force has zero moment arm about itself, so H and V both disappear from that equation.
That single equation solves for T immediately. Then you plug back into force equations to recover H and V. This is the standard trick for rigid-body equilibrium.

STATE_1.description: Identify the hinge as a pin joint and draw the free body diagram of the rod or door, showing weight at the centre of mass.

STATE_2.description: State that the hinge force can act in any direction. Resolve it into unknown H (horizontal) and V (vertical) components at the hinge.

STATE_3.description: Write sum(Fx) = 0 and solve for H. Write sum(Fy) = 0 and solve for V.

STATE_4.description: Combine with Pythagoras: F_hinge = sqrt(H^2 + V^2). Find direction theta = atan2(V, H).

STATE_5.description: State the special case: if there is no external horizontal load, H = 0 and F_hinge reduces to V = W (pure vertical support).

primitives.text: Step 1: Identify the body — a rod pinned at one end by a hinge.
Step 2: Draw the FBD. Mark weight W at the centre of mass (L/2).
Step 3: Mark any external load F_ext at its point of application.
+1 for FBD with correct points
Step 4: Hinge force is unknown in direction — resolve into H and V.
Step 5: Let H act along +x and V act along +y at the hinge.
Step 6: Negative H or V just means the actual direction is opposite to assumed.
+1 for decomposition
Step 7: sum(Fx) = 0  ->  H = F_ext (if F_ext is the only horizontal force).
Step 8: sum(Fy) = 0  ->  V = W (if W is the only vertical force).
Step 9: For multi-unknown problems, add sum(tau)_hinge = 0 to solve external tensions first.
+1 for equilibrium equations
Step 10: Combine H and V using Pythagoras:
Step 11: F_hinge = sqrt(H^2 + V^2).
Step 12: Direction: theta = atan2(V, H) measured from +x axis.
+1 for magnitude and angle
Step 13: Special case — no horizontal load (F_ext = 0).
Step 14: Then H = 0 and V = W, so F_hinge = W (pure vertical).
Step 15: This is the simplest textbook case — real problems usually have H too.
+1 for special-case check

shortcuts.use_when: Once H and V have been found from force equilibrium.

shortcuts.rule: Kill the hinge force by taking torques about the hinge
shortcuts.use_when: Problem has three unknowns (H, V, plus an external tension or normal). Solve the external unknown first, then come back.

shortcuts.rule: Weight of a uniform rod acts at L/2
shortcuts.use_when: Any uniform rod/beam/ladder problem. For non-uniform bodies, use given centre of mass.

shortcuts.rule: Pure vertical hinge when no external H exists
shortcuts.use_when: No external horizontal pull/push or friction is mentioned. Collapses to elementary case.

shortcuts.formula: tan(theta) = V / H  ->  theta = atan2(V, H)
shortcuts.use_when: Exam asks for angle with the vertical/horizontal or with the rod.

shortcuts.formula: Assume +x, +y. Solve. Negative result = assumed direction was wrong.
shortcuts.use_when: You are unsure whether the hinge pushes left or right, up or down.

edge_cases.title: Ladder leaning on a smooth wall, rough floor
edge_cases.trap: Students forget that the smooth wall has zero friction, so only horizontal N from the wall balances the floor friction.
edge_cases.resolution: At the floor: N_floor (up), f_floor (horizontal). At the smooth wall: only N_wall (horizontal). Write force and torque equations. Ladder does not slip when f_floor = N_wall and both satisfy static bound.

edge_cases.title: Crane arm with a rope at an angle
edge_cases.trap: Students resolve the rope tension wrong, leading to incorrect H.
edge_cases.resolution: Rope tension T acts along the rope. Split into T cos(alpha) horizontal and T sin(alpha) vertical. Take torque about the hinge to solve T first; then force equilibrium gives H and V.

edge_cases.title: Hinge actually pushes outward (away from the wall)
edge_cases.trap: Students assume the hinge always pushes toward the wall.
edge_cases.resolution: If the external load is above the hinge pivot level, the hinge can push outward. Trust the algebra — a negative H simply means direction reverses.

edge_cases.title: Door with two hinges
edge_cases.trap: Students treat both hinges as identical and split the load 50/50.
edge_cases.resolution: Take torques about one hinge to find the force on the other. Typically the top hinge pulls the door toward the wall and the bottom hinge pushes it out — opposite horizontal forces.

edge_cases.title: Rod rotating about the hinge (non-equilibrium)
edge_cases.trap: Students use sum(F) = 0 even though the rod accelerates.
edge_cases.resolution: Use Newton's second law for the centre of mass plus torque equation about the hinge: sum(tau) = I_hinge * alpha. Hinge force has centripetal and tangential components.
